data.title: TEGNA: Attractive Amidst Regulatory Uncertainty
data.description:
While shares are up 3% from last year, it has been a bumpy ride for investors in TEGNA Inc. (TGNA). After weak performance in H1 2025 amid fears of an advertising slowdown, the stock popped when Nexstar (NXST) announced a deal to buy the company. Since then, we have seen TEGNA shares steadily drift lower as fears have grown about potential regulatory hurdles to the deal. I last covered shares in August, rating TEGNA a “Buy,” but the stock is down 5% since then. With shares at a meaningful discount to the deal price, now is a good time to revisit TEGNA to see if investors should buy into weakness or exit shares.

NXST Is Paying $22 In Cash For TGNA

First, to recap the terms of the deal, in mid-August, Nexstar agreed to acquire all of TEGNA’s outstanding shares for $22 in cash. Frankly, this was a bit less than I expected, and TGNA’s decision to focus on an all-cash offer may have limited the price somewhat. However, from this point forward, the analysis is more straightforward than if investors were to be receiving NXST stock, whose value can change daily. I would also note that TEGNA shareholders have already approved this deal, removing one potential variable to the successful close of the merger.

With shares closing on Wednesday at $18.91, investors have 16.3% upside to the deal price. Plus, TEGNA can continue to pay its regular dividend, providing a 2.6% yield. The companies are targeting a close in the second half of 2026, so if the deal goes through, this would be a compelling total return opportunity for investors over a 6-9 month window. In a deal with limited regulatory risk, I would expect shares to trade within ~5% of the deal price. Assuming the stock would trade closer to $16-$17 in the immediate aftermath of a failed deal (similar to its pre-deal level), this suggests the market is pricing in just a 50%-60% probability of the deal going through.

FCC Cap Is The Key Uncertainty

That brings us to the regulatory backdrop. In early November, the Department of Justice requested information on the proposed transaction, which is typical for a deal of this magnitude and not something I would reach much into (positively or negatively). Ultimately, the regulator that matters here the most is the FCC because current FCC rules bar a single broadcaster from owning stations that reach more than 39% of households, which the combined company would surpass. Most of the broadcast stocks, like TEGNA, rallied in the aftermath of President Trump’s victory in the 2024 election in the belief his Administration would likely roll back these rules. In fact, Nexstar management speaking at a conference actually said, “we wouldn’t be contemplating this transaction if he [Trump] weren’t in the White House.”

However, this view around deregulation was thrown into uncertainty when the President posted his opposition to lifting the cap in November on Truth Social. Now, the president is very active on social media, and not every post becomes policy. His post also focused on not expanding the broadcast networks. This deal does not change the reach of ABC or NBC—it would simply change who owns the affiliate station. Notably, on Jan. 14, there were reports that the FCC Chairman Brendan Carr was still planning to lift the cap, consistent with his long-standing position, despite the President’s prior post.

Nexstar Is Positioned To Close The Deal, In My View

Now, to be clear, in this article, I am making no political statements or saying what policy is “better” than another; however, in a case like this when a regulatory decision will determine how a stock performs, one must consider the political backdrop. To that end, it is notable that Nexstar was one of the broadcasters that chose to preempt Jimmy Kimmel’s show during the controversy in September, which aligned the network with the Administration. Speaking at the December conference, Nexstar reiterated its belief the deal would be completed. I would also note that the FCC could choose to grant Nexstar a waiver to complete the deal without eliminating the cap entirely.

Fundamentally, there are also clear competitive reasons to remove this cap. This cap was intended to limit concentration of power in media, but the media backdrop is very different today than decades ago. Broadcast networks face competition from cable news, digital outlets, social media, and other platforms. Alphabet’s YouTube can essentially reach every American household, as can Facebook. The risk of a broadcaster having a true information monopoly is very remote in my view, with or without this cap, which is why I believe it will be eventually repealed or diluted.

Termination Fee Provides Some Support

Now, some of these regulatory hurdles are likely to bring back bad memories of long-time TEGNA shareholders because its attempt to sell itself to an arm of Apollo (APO) for $24 failed due to regulatory review deals by the FCC during the Biden Administration. That deal was ultimately terminated, though one offset was that TEGNA received a $136 million breakup fee, which enabled a large share repurchase program. Still, the stock spent much of the time since then stuck in the mid-teens before reaching this deal with Nexstar.

Having gone through this once before, TEGNA insisted upon a termination fee if the deal cannot be completed. Under the merger agreement, Nexstar has to pay TEGNA $125 million if it cannot get regulatory approval by Aug. 18, 2026. Either company has the right to extend this deadline one time for three months, so the true deadline here is Nov. 18, 2026. This payment does help to cushion the potential downside if a deal fails and is ~$0.75/share based on its shares outstanding as of the third quarter.

TGNA Is Cheap Even If The Deal Is Blocked

Now, if the deal gets approved, the outcome for TGNA is clear—shares will be sold to NXST for $22. However, investors must also consider the downside if a deal fails. In Q3, TEGNA earned $0.33, and revenue fell by 19% to $651 million. This large revenue decline is because in 2024 there were the Olympics and a presidential election, which each drive substantial ad revenue. Local networks generate much more revenue in election years, which is why I generally view their financials on a four-year basis, given the calendar year seasonality.

This revenue decline was consistent with my expectations. Distribution revenue fell by 1% to $358 million as consumers continue to cut the cord, partially offset by price increases. Non-political ad revenue fell 12%, primarily due to the Olympics; still, the macro environment has been challenging for the ad market, with companies pulling back on their marketing budgets. Political revenue fell by 92% to $10 million. As a result, EBITDA fell by 52% to $141 million. TEGNA has generated $222 million of free cash flow so far this year.

I was targeting $300 million of free cash flow this year, and I expect TEGNA to come within $15 million of that level, depending on working capital. Essentially, while facing ongoing declines, TEGNA is performing as I expected. TEGNA has suspended issuing guidance, which is typical when a company is being acquired. As a reminder, 2025 is seasonally its worst year in a four-year cycle. 2026 will improve with the midterms; then 2027 will soften, and 2028 will be the strongest (all else equal) due to the presidential election. As such, run-rate free cash is higher than this $300 million, and I see ~$450 million as its average level.

TEGNA ended Q3 with $233 million of cash and 2.9x net leverage. We are likely to see its cash position build over 2026 as it will not be repurchasing stock while waiting for the Nexstar deal to close. If the deal fell through, it would have to added $125 million of cash as well from Nexstar. TEGNA could easily do a ~$300 million buyback immediately, which would reduce the share count by ~10%.

The run-rate business would have ~$2.75-$3.00 of free cash flow, so even with shares at ~$19 today, TEGNA has a 15+% free cash flow yield. While the business is declining, this is very attractive, especially as I see the decline being closer to 2%-3% than 5%, as evidenced by just a 1% drop in distribution revenue. If the deal is blocked, shares would almost certainly fall in a knee-jerk fashion, but even at $19, I actually think TEGNA is positioned to deliver a long-term double-digit return to shareholders.

Shares Are A "Buy"

As such, I think this decline in TEGNA stock has created a compelling opportunity. With FCC Chairman Carr's preference for removing the cap and Nexstar’s political maneuvering, I expect the deal to close and assign a 75% probability. This will give investors an 18% return, including the dividend, over just six to nine months. Even if the deal were to get blocked, TEGNA’s building cash position and termination fee would support a large buyback, and shares are cheap enough, that there is limited long-term downside even if they could briefly dip to $17. I recommend buying TEGNA here and rate shares a “Buy.”

data.title: SiTime: Valuation Is Holding Me Back From Turning Bullish
data.description:
Investment summary

My recommendation for SiTime Corp (SITM) is a hold rating. SITM is a high-quality business in the semiconductor industry, with clear structural tailwinds from AI infrastructure and data centers. However, this strength is already priced in by the market, I believe. While the fundamentals remain excellent, the valuation leaves little room for error. At current levels, the risk-reward is not attractive.

Company Description

SITM offers a strong solution to precision timing. Unlike the legacy method, which was quartz-based, the SITM solution is silicon-native and structurally superior. One way to visualize this is to think of the processor as the brain of an electronic system, and the timing device is the heartbeat. The system will not work without the heartbeat (timing). The constraints with the quartz-based method are that it is fragile, bulky, and limited in flexibility in modern designs.

SITM MEMS-based silicon timing architecture solves all these. By using standard semiconductor manufacturing, SITM delivers programmable timing devices that are smaller and more durable (to heat, vibration, and electromagnetic interference). Operation-wise, SITM is a fabless designer. Its sole focus is purely on performance, customization, and iteration, and not production. With ~90% market share in MEMS timing, SITM is an effective monopoly in this niche segment.

AI & Data Center

SITM should continue to print rapid growth because of its exposure to the AI and data center industry, specifically through its Communications, Enterprise & Data Center segment, which saw revenue grow 115% y/y in Q3 2025 (6th straight quarter of triple-digit growth). The crux here is the AI infrastructure arms race. As data centers scale into massive AI clusters, especially those built around platforms like NVIDIA’s Blackwell (which is extremely expensive), there is no tolerance for timing error. Take a second to think of how complex the entire architecture is. You have thousands of GPUs, switches, and optical interconnects stitched together, and all of them must tick in the same timing. As I said, timing is mission-critical. Even a 1/1000 shift in timing can cascade into huge problems like latency, data corruption, or underutilized compute.

This technical complexity, then, creates a massive opportunity for SITM. In the past, timing in a standard server was cheap and simple. But today, in a fully configured AI rack, the demand for extreme precision is so high that SITM content per rack has jumped to the hundreds of dollars (source: 28th Annual Needham Conference). And I expect this dollar amount to go even higher with the upcoming shift to 1.6T optical modules. Put simply, to move data at that speed, the time window to read each bit of information becomes incredibly tiny. You would need an ultra-precise and steady signal to capture the data. Because these are way more complex and harder to make, I believe it would justify SITM to sell them at much higher prices.

Apple as a Strategic Catalyst

The next growth catalyst is SITM’s relationship with Apple (AAPL). SITM has secured critical design wins for Apple’s internal modem program, which I believe carries significant value for SITM as a business. Most readers are aware of how obsessed AAPL is with saving space inside the iPhone (started by Steve Jobs), and when they decided to build their own internal modem, they hit a physical limit. The traditional quartz timing components were simply too big and fragile to fit into the iPhone (literally cannot fit). Here comes SITM with its MEMS chips that are up to 1,000 times smaller than quartz, which effectively solved this problem. Beyond the fact that this shows MEMS chips can actually replace quartz-based methods, the other notable aspect is that AAPL didn't just put SITM in the iPhone for a minor purpose; they put it in the radio (which is the most sensitive and critical part of the phone). The way I see it, if SITM is good enough for AAPL (one of the largest tech players in the world), it should be able to meet the demand for most other customers.

The financial impact is massive, and the number should become more apparent in SITM’s P&L as this new internal modem rolls across the other iPhone lineups. Therefore, I would say this deal is not really a one-off content win, but one that allows SITM to ride on AAPL's long-term product roadmap. Some might argue that AAPL could easily switch, but I don’t think so. Once a custom timing solution is designed to fit AAPL’s complex architecture, it becomes very sticky. Remember, this isn't a commodity. SITM’s MEMS chips are up to 1,000x smaller than quartz. AAPL has likely designed its entire internal modem board around this; hence, to switch back to a competitor (who would likely use bulkier quartz), AAPL would physically have to redesign the motherboard to find space (that doesn't exist). Furthermore, validating a new timing source would mean a delay for its upcoming products.

The Titan & Aura Expansion

SITM is also expanding its total addressable market, which enhances the long-term growth runway. The launch of the Titan resonator platform is a prime example of how it is doing so. Historically, SITM sold oscillators (this is basically bundled solutions that include both a resonator and a chip), and that left a gap in the whole product (the resonator). With Titan, SITM can now close this gap and address this ~$4 billion market (source: Q3 2025 earnings call). My belief is SITM will win a good chunk of share here since it is already selling the oscillators, and the trust has already been established between it and the customers. But more importantly, the MEMS resonator can be embedded directly inside a customer’s chip package. This is a huge value proposition for semiconductor designers because it removes the need for an external quartz crystal on the circuit board. In turn, this simplifies design layouts and saves space, which is valuable for IoT products (like smartwatches that have strict physical limitations).

This is also margin accretive, as management has indicated Titan products should carry gross margins of >60% (recent gross margin is ~59%). As such, when these products scale, they should lift the overall business margin profile.

The other way SITM is expanding its TAM is by moving up the value chain. Through the Aura Semiconductor acquisition, SITM obtained the necessary IP to offer complete clock systems and not just sell the individual timing components. This improves the relationship with customers, I believe, because SITM effectively becomes a turnkey solution partner that can address a complete solution. There is no need for customers to check out other solution providers (saving time and easy to manage).

Valuation

Overall, I believe there is good visibility that SITM can continue to grow, backed by structural AI and data center spending tailwinds, the APPL catalyst, and TAM expansion. Because of the small earnings base today, I used revenue growth to better value the business. Relative to peers, SITM has the best growth profile, and it makes sense that it trades at a premium. Peers trade on average ~5x forward revenue with low-to-mid-teens revenue growth, while SITM trades close to 19x with >30% revenue CAGR over the next two years.

Using consensus estimates, which have been historically accurate (in fact SITM always beats), SITM should do $700 million in revenue in FY28. The question is what multiple it deserves at that point when the percentage growth has lowered due to the law of large numbers. In FY28, the expected revenue growth rate is in the mid-20%, which is somewhere between Rambus and Semtech. Take the average multiple of the two; at ~9x forward revenue multiple, this implies SITM is worth an enterprise value of $6.3 billion. Add all the cash that SITM is expected to generate (about $230 million or so between Y26 and FY27) and the current cash it has ($809 million), and the market cap sums up to ~$7.4 billion, or a share price of $281. You could argue that SITM deserves to trade at a higher multiple because of what’s going on, but I think this shows that valuation is not cheap, and investors cannot get attractive returns without making aggressive assumptions.

Conclusion

SIM is doing almost everything right operationally. It benefits from secular AI-driven tailwinds, is deeply embedded in Apple’s roadmap, and continues to expand its TAM. What holds me back is not the fundamentals, but the valuation. At ~19x forward revenue, much of this upside seems to be already priced in. I do think SITM is a high-quality compounder, but I recommend a hold rating, waiting for a more attractive entry point.

data.title: Old Republic: Decent Overall Performance, But Still Overvalued Into 2026
data.description:
In this article, I'll be providing an update on my thesis for Old Republic International (ORI), an insurance company, which I last updated on in January of 2025, in an article you can find here. The performance since then for the company has been solid - above the SPY - but recent volatility in the share price in my view confirms my view at the time - that the valuation might have been overvalued.

The past few years have seen a peaking of many insurance companies' valuations, as well as reinsurance company valuations. In general, it's fair to say that I exited many of these investments a bit early, as I seem to want to do in general as well. It's one of my investment shortcomings as an analyst and investor that I try to work on - but it's tricky - because about 30-40% of the time, if I look at my investment volume over the past 5 years, I in fact do not exit too early, but just in time.

Therefore, balancing my tendency to get out too early is hard. With Old Republic, it was, in my view, not as tricky. The reason is that for over 15 years, ORI has been a company that has traded at a relatively discounted valuation, and for a good reason. The reason is that during the GFC and for many years after, the company saw negative AEPS/Earnings. It wasn't actually until 2013 that ORI once again saw positive earnings and "climbed back up". Look below to see an example of how "bad" things were at the time.

This is the problem with some insurance investors/investments on the market today, in my view. They seem to have forgotten the potential volatility of these companies and the underlying markets, despite numerous reminders over the past 10-20 years.

Now, ORI is part of the US P&C market. Because of that, it's fair to say that the company is part of the ongoing bifurcation of the sector.

What does this mean?

That's what this article and update are going to be about - and to show you what this means, you should be careful about the valuation.

Old Republic International - Upside and fundamentals

So, the two main markets in standard insurance are Property & Casualty, P&C, and Life/Health, L&H. Some companies do both, most do only one of the things, or at least focus on the things. The reason for this division is simple - they are different segments with different business models and risk assessment models. P&C is short-term, L&H is a long-term oriented policy, and risk assessments are predictable in L&H, because they use statistics, comorbidity rates with actuarial tables. In P&C, you have to assess based on frequency and severity specific to geographies, areas, and other factors, while also recently taking into consideration things like climate changes (in the industry known as 'catastrophic risk').

For our purpose as investors, P&C is also considered a transactional business. The agents for companies in the sector do get a commission, but also earn based on renewals every time clients pay their premiums. In the L&H sector, it's entirely different - renewals are usually nonexistent, and upfront commissions are comparatively large.

Keep this in mind next time you're negotiating your insurance, and keep the agent's (if there is one) motivations in mind.

Now that we know the difference, what's this bifurcation in P&C?

This means that there's one area which is doing well (depending on your perspective), while another that's doing - to not use an extreme descriptive term - less well.

Property insurance is doing well in the US, where ORI operates. The reasons for this are several. There's significant capital available in the form of both Cat bonds as well as traditional insurance, meaning that there's a battle for "good" risk coverages in many areas. There's an exception to this - natCat areas like the Midwest and the West Coast are seeing persistent high rates for Property insurance, but outside of these, there are significant rate decreases at the point of renewal. This is not necessarily all positive for Old Republic, but it's better than the alternative, below.

Casualty market insurance is not all that well. The US is a very litigious market, and recent large cases that have seen significant jury awards as well as high legal costs are driving up claims and policy costs.

This may be good for some policyowners, but the aggregate effect is a significant rise in costs. For commercial/industrial players, the mere act of getting good coverage has become more complicated due to the need for more "layering" of insurance, as insurance players are moving away from "umbrella" policies. Then there's automotive.

Automotive is, again, especially in the US, going up because of significant medical cost inflation as well as legal cost inflation (as well as repair cost inflation due to complexity). This is despite better safety tech.

So if you want to characterize this market as "not that bad", that's anyone's prerogative - I would consider it to be quite complex, and the thesis for 2026E for any insurance company, but especially a US-based one, to be incredibly complex.

3Q25 was basically a continuation of the company's ongoing income and cost trends - solid income growth, good RoE, and a so-so dividend of 2.7% if you buy at this particular time.

ORI is performing according to its solid business model, but I do not believe it's able to rise above industry trends. Its high valuation is also continually driven by ongoing share buybacks - $44M as of late, with over $900M+ remaining - so expect the inflation in price to remain in part because of this trend. The company's recent results confirm the above trend in premium growth - what between 2016-2021 was relative stable, has risen significantly since 2022. Also, compared to the risks mentioned above, the company is especially exposed to commercial auto, at over 40% of its NPE.

So, overall, I don't view ORI as a "Low risk" investment any longer. This is because the combination of exposure, the market trends and dynamics we're seeing for the 2026-2028E period, as well as this company's significant valuation inflation, driven in part by company actions (such as buybacks), and in part from general valuation trends (the market being priced highly).

Because the full-year results are close (in about a week), I'll also be providing an earnings preview for the company, based on my assumptions for the sector and for the company. Despite the risks and headwinds that I note in both the above and my valuation segment (as well as the separate risks & Considerations), I don't believe that 4Q25 or FY results will bring about anything major. An "as-expected" performance or trend is my current forecast for the company, with a total EPS forecast of $3.3/share for the full year, based on a revenue of about $8.9B, which would come to about ~8% YoY - about $2.3B specifically for 4Q. Main drivers are continued growth in speciality and workers comp, but I expect the title insurance segment to face continued challenging trends related to the above-mentioned challenges in real estate, not only affordability, but also Natcat. I expect this to impact the combined ratio and expect a 20-40 bps decrease (a decrease in the context of combined ratio means an increase to about 95.5% - in combined ratio terms, lower is better). This strengthens my stance that ORI is not a "safer" investment, despite good results.

Valuation trends for ORI, despite a good 3Q25, look as follows:

Valuation for Old Republic International - upside possible

Fundamentals for this company have no similarities to what they once were during their "bad" years. It has a strong bond/stock portfolio, strong reserves, low debt, good capital, and strong management. It will take very strong negative macro trends for the company to "go down" from here. The thing is, the market is setting up for that to be the case (at least potentially).

If we assume that the forecasts are accurate or valid, then we can expect no more than 2-4% growth on average between 2026-2027E. The reason why things might be better than that is that over the last 10 years, the company has been able to beat its forecast estimates most of the time. However, due to a period that's been favorable to most large P&C insurers, this is not exactly a hard feat during those past years. This is one of those scenarios where I'm uncertain about the value of backwards-looking statistics and trends.

The fact is that the company's current valuation is about 12-13x P/E on average. This is next to a historical 5-year average of 10x, about a 20-30% premium, or a 20-year average of 11-12x, about a 5-10% premium. And this is even after the company recently dipped from $46 down to $41/share again, an even clearer overvaluation (in my estimate).

My PT in my last article for ORI came to $27/share, with a fair value of about $37/share. That means that ORI is below my target and FV here. To those that argue that my PT/FV is too low, the fair value represented at the time, and still does, a 2027E 10x P/E, coming to about $36-$37/share. Not only is this, in my estimate, fair given the company's historical risk and automotive exposure, it's also fair given the fact that there are insurance companies on the market that you could buy at significantly lower multiples. Neither the company BV (below $30/share) nor other factors justify a higher valuation to me here.

As of this article, I make an allowance for ORI to outperform further - I raise my FV to an 11x forward P/E, to about $39/share, with a PT of $29/share, but that's about it. If you were to invest here, based on a 5-year average, your potential rate of return would be negative.

Even if you were to forecast at 12-13x P/E, the current valuation, it wouldn't be any higher than single digits. For that reason, I am still at a "HOLD" in my rating here, despite a relatively significant upgrade in my targets and assumptions.

There are risks to investing in Old Republic International - they are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

The US insurance market may be more profitable in some ways, but it also comes with more risks and challenges than the EU market. The accounting for the litigious US legal system, which is driving policy inflation and complexity, is not for the faint-hearted. It doesn't mean that I am no longer investing in US insurance (i have several investments), but it does mean I'm doing so much more conservatively, and only at low multiples.

Risks include, aside from things mentioned above, also things like "The AI of insurance". Many of the bigger players are moving towards operational AI instead of small-scale use of AI for insurance, which means integrating this into the process flow for underwriting and claims adjusting - but this is untested ground. It may fail, or it may rise.

My overall view for the insurance market going into 2026E is not a positive one, especially in the US. Due to the trends mentioned above, I forecast a 5-15% decrease in pricing not only in commercial property, but in other sub-sectors as well. One of the few sectors that's likely to rise, in my view, is workers comp - and because ORI is strong in this sector, this could be an upside to consider (again, at the right price).

Thesis

Old Republic International is an overvalued insurer, albeit one with excellent fundamentals. It has a well-covered yield and low debt, and the results are promising, despite my "HOLD" rating. The company's 20-year history shows a bit of volatility for extended periods of time (especially during the negative period following GFC, several years of underperformance) - but the company has righted and adjusted since, and overall, I view the company as "safe" - at least from a fundamental point of view.

In my previous articles (2024 and 2025), I made it clear that I rotated my position in the Old Republic into what I viewed as better investments. I am still not invested in ORI here. My alternative investments to ORI have performed better than ORI even now, as I've detailed in previous articles, in things like chocolate (Barry Callebaut), and others.

The company is, thanks to recent trends in trading, still overvalued. I increase my PT to $29/share, which still makes it a "HOLD" here as of January 2026. My fair value for the company is at about $39/share, which also makes this company overvalued. I view it as a rotation target.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized):

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic conservative upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which is not enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of $29/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that is not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of January 2026.

data.title: Match Group: Built For The Future At A Decent Value Today
data.description:
Introduction

Match Group, Inc. (MTCH) stock has had a pretty average performance in the last year, with a very modest 1.9% rise. However, over the last five years, the stock's performance has been far less modest, with a decline of 79.5%. Because of this, the company’s shares are now trading at far cheaper levels than they were previously. They only have a forward price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of 14.4x, compared with a five-year average of 32.1. With strong growth expected in the online dating market and Match Group being the leader in the industry, I see this as a great opportunity for investors to gain exposure to this by considering buying some of the company’s shares.

The Growing Online Dating Market

It should come as no surprise to readers that couples are increasingly meeting each other through online dating apps. For example, in the US in 2025, a recent study shows that over 50% of couples met through online dating. This is an increase from 39% in 2017. I anticipate that this will only grow as people spend more time on the internet and online. This is backed up by projections showing that the market is expected to grow by a compounded annual growth rate of 7.5% through to 2034.

Furthermore, as other countries and cultures start adopting online dating, this could boost revenue for the industry. For example, only 4.6% of people globally use dating apps. This could be a great opportunity for the companies behind the industry to take advantage of and to increase user penetration. As the largest online dating company in the world, I believe Match Group is best placed in this field. It owns 4 out of the top 10 most downloaded dating apps, putting it in such a competitively strong place.

Performance And Competitive Strengths

First, let's look at the underlying fundamentals of Match Group. If we look at the company’s latest earnings report, we can see that revenue increased by 2% year-on-year to $914 million in the third quarter of 2025. This is slightly concerning, given that it's lower than the anticipated growth for the industry, suggesting that it’s losing market share. Furthermore, its flagship app and highest source of revenue, Tinder, saw a 3% decline in its revenue to $491 million. These don’t bode well for the company. Moreover, there are concerns about the quality of online dating platforms, with researchers finding that people who met online were less happy than people who met in other ways.

I believe these are big reasons why Match Group has seen its share price fall so much over the last five years. However, not all is as bad as it seems. Its second-largest source of revenue, Hinge, is more than making up for its lost Tinder revenue. Its direct revenue shot up 27% to $185 million. Its paying subscribers also rose by 17% to 187 million. Having Hinge experience such strong growth is far better than having Tinder do so, as the average revenue per paying user (ARPU) of Hinge is $32.87 versus Tinder’s $17.66. Moreover, revenue from Tinder is also stabilizing, growing from $447 million in the first quarter of 2025 to $491 million in the latest quarter. This suggests that the app is in the process of making a turnaround.

From a competitive viewpoint, I believe Match Group still considerably dominates the online dating market scene. From a survey of which dating apps users used in 2025, 37% said they used Tinder, still higher than any other app. 21% used Plenty of Fish, 20% used Hinge, and 18% used Match.com, all also owned by Match Group and included in the top 10. With such a wide variety of platforms to use, I believe this diversification is very useful for the firm to cater for all different users of the platform in terms of what type of relationship they are looking for.

The company spent $50 million in the third quarter to innovate its features and try to expand its global footprint. This is in order to execute its turnaround in 2026 and 2027. If Hinge continues growing strongly, and Tinder continues stabilizing, I think the online dating provider will be in a great position to experience its turnaround, take advantage of its competitive position, and grow strongly as more people use online dating as a way to meet people.

Catalysts For A Turnaround

While the above analysis all sounds well and good, potential investors may be wondering why a turnaround may occur now, given that the outlook of estimates has been looking up for years, and it's still yet to be meaningful for Match Group. This may especially be a significant thought, given that young people are currently experiencing dating app fatigue. For example, 78% of people using dating apps have reported burnout from using them. However, it's important to note that this hasn't led to abandonment. Instead, it has led to users switching between apps. Therefore, those who may be fatigued by the big dating apps, like Tinder and Bumble, are turning to other ones, such as Hinge. This is backed up by the strong growth that Hinge is experiencing.

So while Tinder's growth may be disappointing, the real catalyst for the firm is Hinge. And even then, we can see that Tinder users are stabilizing. This is where Match Group's massive umbrella of dating apps is starting to become useful. Users may leave one Match Group app, like Tinder, but they are joining another, like Hinge. This creates a very effective defensive moat. Crucially, as mentioned above, Hinge's ARPU is much higher than Tinder's, presenting a real opportunity for the firm. Furthermore, the company is focusing on users developing a meaningful connection, such as implementing new features and using AI to improve match relevance. Hinge is already starting to use this, which is why we are seeing it be so successful.

With Hinge's quarterly revenue currently at $185 million, even if it maintained a 20% growth rate over the next two years (well below its current 27%), it could add an extra $326 million in incremental revenue annually. This is almost an additional 10% of revenue. And this is with a conservative estimate for its growth. This will be at a much higher margin, due to Hinge's much higher ARPU.

Moreover, even with dating app fatigue for young people, there's a very good opportunity for Match Group with respect to matchmaking for older generations. This is a demographic that's seeing increasing user numbers, with 12% of over 50's having used apps, rising to 30% for those aged 50-64. Match Group recently had two of its apps, Hinge and Match.com, listed in the top five best dating apps for older people. Older people are also more likely to pay for features on dating apps than younger people, with 44-45% of these users paying for these features compared to just 22% of those under 30. This means they are a more profitable demographic to target. If it can leverage this increasing popularity it has with older people, I think it can help to realise meaningful upside for its stock.

Peer Analysis And Valuation

I’m going to compare Match Group with Bumble Inc. (BMBL), its biggest competitor. Now, on initial inspection, it may look as though Match Group is more expensive than Bumble shares, as its forward P/E of 14.4x is far more than Bumble’s 1.5x. Moreover, Match Group’s forward price-to-sales ratio of 2.2x is also significantly higher than Bumble’s 0.4. However, I believe Match Group is highly deserving of this significant premium to Bumble. Match Group is significantly and consistently profitable, with a net income margin of 16.2%. Bumble, on the other hand, isn’t; it has a net income margin (19.8%). Match Group’s expected revenue growth of 2.2% over the next year is also superior to Bumble’s anticipated decline of 6.7%.

This shows that the company is performing much better than its closest competition. It therefore makes more sense to consider Match Groups' valuation independent of this, too. With the forward P/E of the S&P 500 at 22x, Match Group's forward P/E of 14.4x suggests some undervaluation. The company’s earnings per share are also set to grow by 17-23% over the next three years as well. This means its P/E in 2028 should fall to 8.3x based on current prices. Ultimately, I believe these points contribute to the undervaluation of the firm's shares.

Takeaway

My ultimate reasoning for Match Group’s shares being a good one for investors to consider is that the online dating market is set to grow strongly over the next decade, and that the company’s shares are cheap. I believe that because of growth drivers such as Hinge, it should be able to achieve its turnaround and grow well along with the rest of the industry. Its excellent competitive position and diversification should help it achieve this. Moreover, its shares have fallen significantly over the last five years, providing investors with an excellent price entry point to gain exposure to the online dating market through its shares.

data.title: Microsoft: The Ride Couldn't Last Forever
data.description:
I think there's a strong case to be made that Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) is one of the best and most innovative businesses ever created. I've been lucky enough to participate in its growth over the years. I've also been very bullish about it since the first time I covered it (rating it a Strong Buy at the time).

I've kept a bullish outlook on Microsoft for quite some time, but that's changed in July 2025. That's when I downgraded MSFT to a Hold - I argued that MSFT's fundamentals, while as great as ever, were not catching up with the growth of its valuation. In other words, I found MSFT's price tag pretty hefty and didn't want to add anymore.

That's what I've done ever since. I have not added, but I haven't sold any of my shares either. I've simply watched MSFT underperform the broad S&P 500 Index (SPY) as it generated a negative 9.4% return vs. the S&P 500's positive 9.3%.

As it's around 6 months later now, I have more financial data available, and MSFT's valuation has actually moved towards the direction I expected, I decided to prepare a follow-up coverage.

The business? As strong as ever with Azure driving the growth

As MSFT's shareholder, I was very happy to see a high growth on year-over-year basis and I wasn't surprised that it had been driven primarily by the Intelligent Cloud segment. I saw the segment grew by ~28% y/o/y, which continues to amaze me, especially as MSFT's overall profitability improved as well - operating income margin improved by 200 bps to 49%.

As the management explained during the Earnings Call, the growth in the Intelligent Cloud segment was realized by an outstanding performance of Azure (scoring one of the largest growth pace across the cloud sector):

Next, the Intelligent Cloud segment. Revenue was $30.9 billion and grew 28% and 27% in constant currency. In Azure and other Cloud services, where we continue to see accelerating demand, revenue grew 40% and 39% in constant currency. Results were ahead of expectations, driven by better-than-expected growth in our core infrastructure business, primarily from our largest customers. Azure AI services revenue was generally in line with expectations, and this quarter, demand again exceeded supply across workloads, even as we brought more capacity online.

Many investors express concern about MSFT's heavy capital spending on computing capacity (Need an example? Check out the comment section below my latest article about Nvidia (NVDA)) - I understand that these numbers can be overwhelming, but the growth in the Intelligent Cloud segment speaks for itself. I'd like to remind you that Azure is the second largest environment in the cloud market, falling second to just Amazon's (AMZN) AWS. Still, it keeps growing faster than its larger peer, so it I think it has a chance to close that gap or at least narrow it in the years to come.

I've also seen a lot of noise around MSFT's AI products' growth slowing down during the last two months, but MSFT took a stance on that. As reported by Reuters:

Microsoft on Wednesday denied a report from The Information that multiple divisions at the company lowered sales growth targets for certain artificial intelligence products after several sales staff missed their goals in the fiscal year that ended in June.

(...)

"The Information's story inaccurately combines the concepts of growth and sales quotas, which shows their lack of understanding of the way a sales organization works and is compensated," a company spokesperson said in a statement.

"Aggregate sales quotas for AI products have not been lowered, as we informed them prior to publication."

In fact, I saw quite the outlook of the MSFT's management on potential CAPEX in 2026 - as I quoted earlier, they keep increasing their capacity quarter by quarter and still, the demand keeps outpacing their supply. I think that's the key reason that should keep investors away from falling into oversimplified statements (such as "AI bubble") and truly appreciate that MSFT's CAPEX will allow the Company to have a rightful place in the ongoing AI revolution. I'm pasting the management brief addition on this below:

With accelerating demand and a growing RPO balance, we're increasing our spend on GPUs and CPUs. Therefore, total spend will increase sequentially, and we now expect the FY '26 growth rate to be higher than FY '25. As a reminder, there can be quarterly spend variability from cloud infrastructure build-outs and the timing of delivery of finance leases.

The business isn't the issue - the price is

I won't sugarcoat it - MSFT is not the most attractive total return opportunity out there - why is that? How can it not be attractive given the strength and long-term prospects of its business? As I often say - there are two sides to this coin called "investing." I don't look solely at the fundamentals of the business, but also at its price, which is equally important element of Seeking Alpha.

At first sight, I don't think Microsoft looks overvalued or anything. It's not like its forward-looking P/E multiple is not reasonable - if I were to judge just by the chart below, I would not be overly concerned.

But, I like to put things into perspective. Have you ever thought why some businesses trade at higher and some at lower multiples? One of the reasons (one of the key reasons) is expected growth. For instance, I see that MSFT trades at the P/E of 28x and is expected to growth its top-line by 16%.

I compared it to a few other Mega Cap businesses and here's what I found out. I think Amazon and Alphabet (GOOG) trade at a similar valuation multiple (P/E ratio of 30x and 29.5x, respectively). At the same time, I see the market expects Amazon to grow at a 24.5% pace while GOOG is expected to grow at over 30% y/o/y.

What's the conclusion of that comparison? I think it shows that MSFT doesn't deserve such a high valuation multiple as there are other large businesses that are expected to grow faster, yet trade at similar valuations. I value that comparison as both Amazon and Alphabet have competitive segments to MSFT's key segment - Intelligent Cloud.

I also included Meta (META) as it shows the widest gap - much lower P/E ratio (20x) and the highest growth expectations out of all businesses included in the comparison (nearly 43%).

Will Microsoft selloff?

Nobody has a crystal orb, so I won't be pretending to own one - I don't know if it will sell-off. I think that possibility certainly is on the table, but that's just my interpretation of valuation multiples shared above. For instance, I see that Wall Street Analysts have a different outlook and they expect (on average) over 36% stock price increase from MSFT:

I have a deep respect for MSFT's team, history, and business fundamentals. I want to keep it in my portfolio. Still, with the current valuations and concerns shared above, I don't think MSFT offers an attractive entry-point at these levels. In fact, I think it's quite risky to keep buying it right now.

That's why MSFT remains a Hold for me, but make no mistake - I would be more than happy to see it sell off, as I would certainly increase my stake in that case.

data.title: ATI Inc.: Why Aerospace And Defense Growth Makes This A Strong Buy
data.description:
ATI Inc. (NYSE:ATI) is a U.S. specialty materials company focused on high-performance metals for aerospace, defense, energy, and industrial markets. I am always looking to expand my coverage in the aerospace and defense industries and ATI has been a name that has been long missing from my coverage list. With this report, I am adding ATI to my aerospace and defense coverage which is set to benefit from growing demand in commercial, defense and space markets providing an attractive growth opportunity for investors.

ATI’s Materials Are Pivotal To The Aerospace And Defense Industry

In some way everything stands or falls with materials. Engineers can make designs, which already includes assumptions on what materials to use. To manufacture those designs, high quality alloys are required. Aerospace materials are some of the highest quality materials available adhering to strict requirements. So, it is not a business that is easy to enter and requires a specialty production system.

ATI provides titanium alloys, nickel-based superalloys, isothermal and forged components, hot-section engine materials and near-net shape forgings. With those material products suppliers can make jet engine rotating parts, disks, shafts, rings, landing gear structures, airframe structures and space and defense hardware. So, ATI’s products are of paramount importance, especially to manufacture jet engines which are running at substantially higher temperatures these days to make them more efficient and that requires high quality parts, which requires high quality materials. The company also provides stainless steel, electrical steel and specialty alloys which can be applied in energy, chemical processing, electronics, medical and industrial markets.

The company generates around 70% of its sales from aerospace and defense with the remaining 30% of the sales generated in the energy and industrial market. ATI has two operating segments namely, HPMC and AA&S. HPMC stands for High Performance Materials and Components mostly focused on aerospace and defense while AA&S stands for Advanced Alloys & Solutions focusing on the remaining markets.

The Growth Drivers For ATI

Unsurprisingly, the growth engine of ATI is the aerospace and defense market. Jet engine sales are expected to increase more than 20% this year driven by increasing airplane production rates and strong demand from the MRO (Maintenance, Repair & Overhaul) markets. Airframe sales are expected to increase modestly at 1-2% despite increases in the production rates for the Boeing 737 and Airbus A320. So, what we are seeing is that the within aerospace and defense ATI derives most of its revenues and growth from jet engines. The defense market is expected to see more than 10% growth driven by higher defense spending.

The remaining markets are expected to be down with broad-based weakness, but for specialty energy and electronics the market is expected to improve over the next 18 months. So, some of the growth we are seeing in aerospace and defense is offset by weaker performance in other industries. The weakness is driven by cyclicality and project-based spending in the oil & gas sector, softer demand from China, inventory rationalization in the medical market and capex delays and destocking for industrial customers.

Strong Margin Expansion For ATI

ATI’s sales rose 7% in the third quarter driven by growth in the aerospace and defense industries with particularly strong growth in jet engine and defense sales. EBITDA grew 21% to $225 million bringing the margins to 20%.

HPMC revenues grew 9% to $303 with margins climbing 190 basis points to 24% resulting in 18% EBITDA growth to $146 million. AA&S revenues rose 5% with a 23% increase in EBITDA to $90 million bringing the margins to 17% marking a 250 basis point increase.

Aerospace And Defense Strength Is Here To Stay

For the fourth quarter, the company expects adjusted EPS of $0.84-$0.90 and adjusted EBITDA of $221-$231 million driven by jet engine and defense strength and some airframe growth shifting into 2026 as production rate increase timing is shifting to the right. For the full year, the company expects $3.15-$3.21 in earnings per share which is a $0.14-$0.25 lift to the guidance. EBITDA guidance improved $38 million at the low end and $18 million at the high end. Free cash flow guidance also increased with the current range now being $330-$370 million.

ATI Stock Is A Strong Buy With Optionality For More Repurchases

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For ATI, I am expecting a CAGR of 15% on EBITDA driven by growing sales in the aerospace and defense business and 47.5% growth in free cash flow. The company has a healthy net debt leverage, which I believe would also allow the company to repurchase more shares than I have currently modeled. For ATI, I am initiating coverage with a strong buy rating and a $139.78 base case price target indicating 13% upside and a $163.13 price target in an extremely bullish market setting. We do note that the company is trading substantially above its median valuation levels, but I do believe that as the company grows its aerospace and defense business the 10.4x EV/EBITDA median is not representative of a proper valuation base.

As aerospace and defense sales continue to grow, I expect the company to show growth of 7.3% annually and EBITDA growth to outpace sales growth leading to margins climbing to north of 20%. Cash conversion ratio, which is free cash flow divided by EBITDA, is expected to improve as well while CapEx as a percentage of operating cash flow is expected to come down. I do expect the company to repurchase around $275-$300 million in shares per year while maintaining its cash balance. For 2025, the company did have the Allegheny Ludlum 6.95% Debenture maturing which limits the space for repurchases. The company had $700-$750 million in cash in 2023 and 2024. I believe that the company does not require such a cash balance and could also operate on a cash balance of around $350 million. I have assigned the excess cash to repurchases and note that the company’s leverage is set to improve from around 1.6x to 0.9x what this essentially means is that by 2027 there is another $770 million available for repurchase driving additional upside to the 2027 price targets.

Conclusion: ATI Has Substantial Growth Ahead Supporting Higher Share Prices

I believe that ATI’s growth in aerospace and defense will substantially drive the EBITDA and free cash flow performance unlocking additional upside. We note that the company is seeing lower sales in other markets, but aerospace and defense growth will dictate the company’s performance and upside. ATI's stock has risen over 100% in the past year, but I believe that an aerospace and defense peer group valuation would indicate even more upside and warrant a strong buy rating. What would further amplify my bullish view on the company is if ATI would ultimately assign a portion of its capital to pay dividends creating the opportunity to attract long-term oriented investors with a strong yield-on-cost opportunity.

data.title: Imperial Petroleum: A Share Issuing Machine, Initiating Strong Sell
data.description:
Imperial Petroleum Inc. (IMPP) is a company that operates a small fleet of tanker and dry bulk ships. Currently, it is trading at a significant discount to its assets.

I believe that this discount is more than justified, following what can only be described as a series of poor managerial decisions.

Misleadingly Cheap

Upon looking at the balance sheet, one might think this is a great investment opportunity. After all, the company has nearly $172 million in cash and short-term investments while owning a modest fleet, which has a net book value of $343.1 million as per the Q3 2025 filing.

On top of that, the company is nearly debt free and is shown as having an attractive P/E and Market Cap as shown by the picture below.

To the un-initiated, this looks like a once in a lifetime opportunity and initially I personally thought the same and wanted to invest in the company. However, upon further investigation, I have found more than enough reason to stay away.

Dilution, Dilution, Dilution

One quick look at the history of this company's shares reveals a different story. One of significant and systemic dilution of shareholders. To put things into perspective, I will grab the outstanding share count from 2022, 2023, 2024 and 2025 (grabbed from the recent equity raise prospectus). I will then showcase the annual growth rate to see how diluted your average shareholder would get over that period. Note that numbers are adjusted for the 1-15 reverse share split that was announced in April 26, 2023.

Year 2022 2023 2024 2025
Shares Outstanding (in millions) 12.97 29.81 34.02 45.75
Annual Growth Rate N/A 130% 14% 34%

Clearly there is a pattern of strong dilution, which would be fine if this was met with significant earnings and asset expansion. I will show a chart of fleet size by dwt to see if the capital raised is being allocated appropriately.

Year 2022 2023 2024 2025
Fleet Size (in thousands of dwt) 808 791 751 1195
Annual Growth Rate N/A -2% -5% 59%

Just to ensure we have a total view of what has happened with dilution and fleet growth, we can take a look at the difference between 2022 and the latest figures. Shares outstanding up 253%, fleet growth up only 48%.

Operational Performance

Naturally, one might say that revenues have actually climbed from 2022, highlighting a counterpoint to my claim that capital was misallocated. Naturally, I will examine this claim and determine if it has a solid ground to stand on.

With $221.5 million spent in capital expenditure, we can grab figures from 2022, 2023 and 2024 to determine if at current, that spending was justified. Note that for 2025, I will be using the Q3 2025 figure and annualizing it.

Year 2022 2023 2024 2025
Revenue 97.0 183.7 147.5 165.6
Annual Change N/A 89% -20% 12%

From what we can see, an expenditure of $221.5 million has actually grown the company's revenues by $68.6 million (or 71%) since 2022, quite impressive.

Yet if we compare this to revenue per share (by grabbing the previous share counts and dividing them by said revenue figures) we see a different story.

Year 2022 2023 2024 2025
Revenue/Share 7.48 6.16 4.34 3.62
Annual Change N/A -18% -30% -17%

As can be seen, dilution overcomes any significant revenue growth with revenue per share dropping consistently year over year. The total revenue per share change has been -52% from 2022 to present.

On top of that, we can see that operational fleetwide utilization has been poor sitting below 85% in 2022 and below 80% in 2023 and 2024. This means that a significant chunk of potential revenues are simply being wasted. There is no excuse for this and despite stronger data in Q3 2025 (88.7% operational utilization), this does not inspire confidence in the operation of the fleet as other peers typically report fleet utilization figures of over 90%.

Related Party Transactions

While the above section alone could simply be written off as unfortunate timing, I believe that this section will show a different story.

Looking at the company's latest 20-F filing, you can see a section entitled "Vessel Acquisitions" which showcases their vessel transactions.

You will note that all of these transactions seem to have a certain caveat to them. That being who the counterparty of the transaction is. In all cases, it is either entities owned by Mr. Harry Vafias, or the Vafias family. This allows Mr. Vafias' private entities such as Brave Maritime Corp. Inc. to be used as a way to effectively transfer operational risk onto shareholders while getting a significant pay out in return.

The fact that the transaction was approved by the company's audit committee does not ease my mind one bit, as it does not change the clear conflicts of interest presented.

In My Opinion, Warrants Prevent Upside

I believe that even if you have reason to believe that the company's management is planning to reward shareholders in the future and that sector fundamentals are positive, you will not get the same return as you could with other companies in the same sector.

Why is that?

This is due to the numerous (over 9.52 million each) Class F and G warrants issued by the company in their recent offering, which place an effective cap on the share price at $6.30 a share (the exercise price of said warrants).

Ignoring the various classes of warrants that are currently way out of the money and likely will never be exercised, there are currently 3.17 million Class E warrants with an exercise price of $2.00 a share, 0.71 million shares issuable at $3.07 a share as part of stock options and 1.65 million shares issuable as part of an acquisition of 3 bulker ships.

The current common share count of 45.75 million excludes these according to the company's statement below.

Considering the above, this represents imminent dilution of 5.53 million shares at current market prices, representing dilution of 12% or a "true" share count of 51.28 million shares.

If market prices were to reach above $6.30 a share, we can assume an additional dilution of around 19.04 million shares effectively capping prices at slightly above $6.30 a share.

Management Can't Change

Clearly a pattern seems to be present with this management team, and you may be asking yourself "Why hasn't management been changed?".

Unfortunately for investors in this company, Mr. Harry Vafias has made sure he has 49.99% voting power through his purchase of 16,000 Series B Preferred Shares. These shares grant Mr. Harry Vafias 25,000 votes per share, granting near total control over the company. The only reason why he does not have majority voting power is because of a clause that ensures that no single person may have more than 49.99% of the total voting power in the company.

Nonetheless, this can be seen as complete power over the company as the other shareholders (of which retail is a large percentage of) cannot meaningfully organize to get a majority of votes against the CEO.

Conclusion and Key Takeaways

Overall, this is a classic example of a value trap. However, I will say that there is one main risk to my thesis.

That being the fact that management may very well prove me wrong and decide to turn over a new leaf and be more shareholder friendly. Such has not happened throughout the history of the company, however, I cannot predict the future or what management may be thinking so this is not a risk I can control.

I set my rating at strong sell and consider the company to be completely uninvestible investment in its current form, and call for investors to stay away from this company.

data.title: Ryder System: Cheap Valuation With A Better Earnings Profile Vs. The Previous Peak
data.description:
Investment action

I give a buy rating for Ryder System (R) because of its private fleet outsourcing growth driver, technology advantage, inflation-protected contracts, and better earnings profile. At the same time, valuation remains cheap.

Company description

R's main business is Fleet Management Solutions [FMS], where it owns and manages fleets end-to-end (from maintenance to compliance, fueling, and ensuring uptime). Essentially, R enables customers to offload operational risk entirely. R also has an asset-light business called Supply Chain Solutions [SCS], where R designs and runs logistics networks. Lastly, R also offers Dedicated Transportation Solutions [DTS] that act as a private fleet for customers (includes equipment, drivers, and routing).

Secular Tailwinds

There is a strong secular tailwind that is driving R’s growth, and that is the sheer scale of the private fleet outsourcing opportunity. To put things in perspective, the US and Canada operate ~4.4 million private trucks. Most of these trucks are owned by companies to meet their own needs (be it selling groceries, making industrial goods, or running retail logistical networks). They are not competent in managing drivers, maintaining the engines, or even navigating the regulatory environment. The problem is as compliance burdens increase and maintenance costs go up, many of these operators are stuck with their private fleets becoming liabilities, not assets. This is where R comes into play. R gives these operators a way to let go of these “liabilities” by converting their in-house fleets into dedicated or leasing contracts. For the customers, this is a clean, lower-risk, and cheaper solution (from a total cost of ownership perspective). Once R controls the fleet, it becomes deeply embedded and very hard to replace.

What really locks this in, though, is the technology, I believe. In the past, logistics was really a black box. Shippers would hand off the freight and basically lose visibility until it arrived at the destination. If a delay happened, they would only find out when it’s too late to salvage the situation. With R, the entire lifecycle becomes transparent through its platforms like RyderShare and RyderGyde. RyderShare gives customers real-time visibility into freight movement across the supply chain. RyderGyde lets fleet managers schedule maintenance, monitor vehicle health, and manage operations from a smartphone (think about the efficiency of this). And we know these tech upgrades are driving conversion because management disclosed that ~1/3 of new dedicated wins are directly driven by them. This shifts the growth narrative to a more structural one because R is not only just competing purely on price (lower total cost of ownership) but also offering a structurally better solution.

Profitability and Continuity

R's revenue model is largely defensive because of its contract structure. ~90% of revenue is contractual, with average terms between three and seven years and built-in inflation pass-through mechanisms. As such, be it wages or maintenance costs that increase, those costs are passed directly to the customer, which is a huge differentiator versus spot-market carriers who basically are at the mercy of daily freight rates. Combined with the structural tailwinds I mentioned, I believe R’s financials are decoupling from the general cycle.

The numbers say it all. It is known that North America is undergoing a freight downturn, but yet R is generating an ROE of ~17%. Compared to the 2018 peak cycle, R only delivered ~13% ROE. Have a think about what that implies. This tells me that R is earning materially higher returns today, in a weak market, than it did in the best market of the prior cycle. Flipping the equation around, if R can earn mid-to-high teens ROE in a downturn, it should do significantly better during an upturn. And my view is the cycle will eventually turn. When it does, we should see strong operating leverage from higher rental rates (the cost of vehicles is fixed anyway) and used vehicle values (this makes depreciation cost per unit lower, and hence, higher margins).

The final piece that gives me confidence R can continue to operate at this level is the planned CEO transition. Robert Sanchez will be moving to Executive Chairman, and John Diez will step in as CEO. For one, this removes a major source of uncertainty. Two, Diez is not a new hire. He was the guy that ran FMS and DTS and served as CFO. In other words, he is literally one of the core guys that helped shape R’s business model today. If I had to pick one CEO for R, Diez is the best guy, in my opinion.

Valuation

Valuation looks cheap to me at 13x forward PE today. You don’t need to make aggressive assumptions to get a nice return from here. Assuming the trough of this cycle is over and R starts to see a very gradual recovery over the next 3 years (revenue growth slowly goes up to mid-single-digit percentage), and net margin recovers to just barely below 2023 levels, R should easily do $675 million in adj. net income. As mentioned, R should have a better ROE than today, and if we benchmark against the previous peak, where R delivered ~13% ROE back in 2018 and traded at ~17x forward PE (late 2017), assuming R to trade at 18x in my model is not extreme.

Risk

The investment case is not without risk. The most immediate risk is a prolonged freight downcycle. If the "lower for longer" environment persists through 2026 or even into 2027, the recovery timeline will be pushed back. There is also the regulatory uncertainty regarding EPA 2027 emissions standards that could delay equipment purchasing cycles, which could hurt demand for R solutions.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating for R because of the structural growth drivers, contract-backed revenue, and strong ROE performance. My core belief is that this freight cycle will eventually turn, and when that happens, R should have a clear path to higher earnings. At today’s valuation, the upside is very attractive even if we assume conservative assumptions.

data.title: Novo Nordisk: Valuation Alone Doesn't Justify Bullishness
data.description:
Generally speaking, I shy away from pharmaceutical companies. But there is a big exception to this. And that would be those involved in the weight loss industry. This is because, in part, I have done a lot of independent research in this space because I ultimately decided, some time ago, to begin taking one of these drugs. And that is none other than Zepbound, which is owned by Eli Lilly and Company (LLY). In the time that I have taken it, I have lost 132 pounds, with 84.4 of those pounds coming off in 2025. So for me, there is a personal angle to this. But up to this point, my focus has primarily been on Eli Lilly and Company.

I figured, coming into 2026, it would be a good idea to spread my wings further. And the next logical candidate to touch on is Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO), the maker of both Ozempic and Wegovy. What I see here is an interesting business. It's actually growing at a nice pace, and generates a lot of profitability and cash flow. Even though its weight loss drugs are expanding rapidly, there are certain competitive pressures that need to be taken into consideration. So even though the stock is cheap on an absolute basis, I think that it makes for a better Hold candidate than it does anything else.

Checking Out Novo Nordisk

Although the pharmaceutical industry has developed a rather poor reputation over the last several decades, a reputation that is not necessarily unearned, there have been positive aspects of it. The fact of the matter is that humans are living longer now than virtually any other time in history. And while there have been multiple contributors to that, such as technological improvements, government spending initiatives, and the like, drugs have certainly been a key component. The management team at Novo Nordisk specifically mentions in their 2024 annual report that the global prevalence of serious chronic diseases remains a problem for millions of people. That year, the company served over 45.2 million people globally, making it a true powerhouse in this space. And because of the drugs that it focuses on, it can do well financially by doing good.

When I say that is a global enterprise, I mean it. The company has research and development facilities in five countries. It has production facilities in 13 countries. And it has 80 countries with affiliates. Primarily, the business focuses on producing drugs centered around addressing diabetes and obesity. The goal here is not just maintenance, but also providing a cure. I already mentioned two of these. They are Ozempic and Wegovy. Ozempic is specifically approved to treat Type 2 diabetes. It does this by lowering blood sugar and is believed to reduce certain risks, like those centered around heart attack, stroke, and more. Meanwhile, Wegovy is approved for chronic weight management and is essentially a higher dose of semaglutide aimed at helping individuals lose weight. There are, of course, others. The oral semaglutide tablet that Novo Nordisk sells is called Rybelsus and its purpose is to treat diabetes as well. But outside of this, the company has other drugs that it provides.

Examples include Resiba, Victoza, Ryzodeg, Xultophy, and Fiasp for diabetes, as well as Saxenda for obesity. But, the company also engages in the rare disease space. For instance, NovoSeven RT is a recombinant factor VIIa that helps to stop bleeding and prevent bleeding during surgeries for individuals that suffer from certain types of hemophilia. NovoEight is a recombinant factor VIII replacement therapy that treats and prevents bleeding episodes for people with hemophilia A. There are other examples as well. For instance, its Rivfloza is an RNA interference therapy that treats Primary Hyperoxaluria Type 1, which is a genetic liver condition that leads to recurrent kidney stones and kidney damage over time. However, it is worth noting that all of these rare disease treatments represent only a small piece of the overall pie for the company.

Over the last few years, Novo Nordisk has achieved remarkable growth. In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the 2022 through 2024 fiscal years, while in the chart below, you can see results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. It is important to note here that all of these figures are based in U.S. dollars on a constant currency basis. This is necessary because the company actually reports its financial results in Danish krone. Seeing as how I think in dollars, and how I imagine most of my readers doing the same, I did take this liberty when analyzing the company.

With that caveat in mind, you can see how, in the aforementioned charts, financial performance for the business has exploded in recent years. Even in 2025, the picture for the firm was improving rapidly. Revenue of $36.08 billion in the first nine months ended up being 12.3% above the $32.12 billion the company generated the previous year. Net profits, operating cash flows, adjusted operating cash flows, and EBITDA, or increased nicely on a year-over-year basis.

As the chart above illustrates, a lot of the growth that the company saw has come from the success of its Ozempic and Wegovy brands. The latter of these has been particularly amazing. In 2022, it generated sales of only $971 million. But by 2024, that revenue had jumped to $9.13 billion. For the first nine months of 2025, revenue was $8.98 billion. That happened to be 49.3% above the $6.02 billion reported the previous year.

Long term, management believes that there is an opportunity for significant value here. According to the company, there are over 550 million people across the globe who have either Type 1 or Type 2 diabetes. And over 900 million people are living with obesity. Even within these groups, there are smaller subcategories that overlap. This includes 250 million people living with MASH (metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis), which is a progressive liver disease whereby it accumulates in the liver, eventually leading to cirrhosis or liver cancer in some patients. There are over 500 million people living with cardiovascular disease. And there are more than 800 million living with chronic kidney disease. To the extent to which these overlap with obesity and diabetes, the current drugs that the company has can prove helpful. But management continues to make investments in these other markets as well with the hope of expanding its revenue over time.

Normally, you would expect financial performance for the business to justify high trading multiples. After all, as I have detailed in articles about Eli Lilly and Company, that firm is trading at astronomical multiples. But with Novo Nordisk, the picture is different. Based on the data that we have seen already for 2025, for the year as a whole, we should likely expect net profits of about $16.45 billion. Adjusted operating cash flow would be about $22.33 billion, while EBITDA would come out to roughly $24.63 billion.

In the chart above, you can see what this means for the valuation of the company. That chart also shows how the company is valued compared to its results for 2024. Meanwhile, in the chart below, you can see how this stacks up against five other similar firms. On a price to earnings basis, three of the five firms ended up being cheaper than it is. But when it comes to the other approaches, the firm is definitely on the cheaper end of the spectrum. Only two are cheaper when it comes to the EV to EBITDA approach, while it ended up being the cheapest when it came to the price to operating cash flow approach.

Considering the rapid growth that we are seeing in this space, and the success that the company has had already, I would normally assign it a Buy rating. But there are some other issues that I am concerned with. And this primarily centers around competitive pressures. In the image below, you can see the global market share of GLP-1 drugs over time. On the whole, Novo Nordisk has unfortunately seen a decline over the last year.

The only reason why revenue continues to increase is because the market itself is growing rapidly. Make the U.S. branded anti-obesity medication market as an example. As one of the most obese nations on the planet, the U.S. is the place where companies like Novo Nordisk should be fighting for the most. But while overall scripts have skyrocketed over the last couple of years from around 100,000 to around 757,000, Wegovy itself has increased only slightly from around 100,000 to 271,000.

The problem here would be other competitive pressures. Unfortunately for Novo Nordisk, the company has seen Eli Lilly and Company really thrive as of late, with its market share expanding at a rapid pace. In its own investor presentation, Eli Lilly and Company specifically showed how common in the U.S. incretin analogs market, its market share has grown even as Novo Nordisk’s market share has fallen rapidly.

And it's not just the drugs that are out today. It's the drugs that will come out tomorrow. This includes its upcoming triple agonist drug called Retatrutide. On December 11th, of this year, Eli Lilly and Company announced new test results for it. It showed weight loss of up to an average of 28.7%, or about 71.2 pounds in total, all achieved over the span of only 68 weeks. And we also have Orforglipron, which will be its first small-molecule GLP-1 receptor agonist that is on its way to market.

This certainly poses a competitive risk for the company. However, it is worth noting that it continues to work on its own pipeline of drugs. In the image above, you can see its existing pipeline, including its own oral semaglutide. The company is also working on its own triple agonist drug, which could prove a success. But as of now, it's clear to me that Eli Lilly and Company is currently winning this arms race. This doesn't mean that the company doesn't have some things going for it. In December of 2025, the FDA approved the pill form of Wegovy, which the firm launched earlier this month for just $149 per month for the lowest doses and for $299 per month for the highest. Not even Eli Lilly and Company's Orforglipron has received approval as of this writing. This could be a space for the company to compete more effectively.

Takeaway

As much as I would love to be bullish about Novo Nordisk, I can't right now. If I were basing this on valuation alone, I would say that this is a compelling opportunity. But with competitive pressures proving to be an issue, the stock is not cheap enough to justify anything better than a Hold rating at this moment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: EDIV: High Concentration, High Risk, Poor Long-Term Prospects
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The SPDR® S&P Emerging Markets Dividend ETF (EDIV) aims to provide exposure to high-yielding emerging market stocks. Historically, this is a strategy which has offered relatively poor long-term results.
